name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Perdona, se me pego (congelo) el programa.

sideB.text: Sorry, my program froze.
My apologies, the program crashed on me.
Sorry, the program got stuck.
Sorry, the program got frozen.

cards.note: ~Sorry, the program got froze~ and ~Sorry, the program got freeze~ are not valid because the past participle of 'freeze' is [frozen]. You can say it [got frozen], but it is much more natural to just say it [froze].

cards.sideA: Lo normal seria que mientras esta en produccion, le agregUemos esta feature.

sideB.text: Normally, while it is in production, we would add this feature.
The usual thing would be to add this feature while it's in production.
Ideally, we would add this feature while it is in production.
The normal way would be to add this feature while it is in production.

cards.note: ~The normal would be~ is a direct translation from Spanish. In English, use an adverb like [Normally] or add a noun like [The normal thing would be].

cards.sideA: Produccion en la base de datos suele cashear las busquedas y ser mas rapida.

sideB.text: The production database usually caches queries and is faster.
Production in the database tends to cache the searches and be faster.
The database in production usually caches the searches, making it faster.

cards.note:
~The database in production use to cache searching~ is not valid because 'use to' implies a past habit that no longer happens. For current routines, you should use [usually caches] or [tends to cache].

~Production in the database use to cache searching~ is incorrect because the subject is reversed. It should be the [database in production].

~Production in the database tends to be cached for queries and be faster.~ is not valid because 'Production in the database' has the reversed subject, and 'tends to be cached' incorrectly implies the database itself is cached, rather than the database [caching the queries].

~Production in the database uses to cache the searches and be faster.~ is not valid because 'uses to' is incorrect for describing current habits or routines. You must use adverbs of frequency like [usually caches] or verbs like [tends to cache].

~Production in the database tends to cache searching and be faster.~ is not valid because using the gerund 'searching' sounds unnatural here; it is better to say cache [searches] or cache [queries]. Also, keep in mind that [The production database] is a much more natural subject.

cards.sideA: …pero para el otro dejarlo como ya es.

sideB.text: ...but for the other one, leave it as it is.
...but leave the other one the way it already is.
...but as for the other one, keep it the same.
...but for the other one, leave it how it is.
...but for the other, leave it as it is.

cards.note: ~...but for the other one, let it as it is.~ is not valid because the correct verb for this fixed expression is 'leave', not 'let'. You should say [leave it as it is].

cards.sideA: El fallback del banner es una imagen local guardada.

sideB.text: The banner's fallback is a locally stored image.
The banner's fallback is a saved local image.
The fallback for the banner is a locally saved image.
The fallback for the banner is a local saved image.
The fallback of the banner is a locally saved image.
The banner fallback is a locally stored image.

cards.sideA: Me encargué de cambiar el link y poner una imagen en el código como fallback.

sideB.text: I took care of changing the link and putting an image in the code as a fallback.
I handled changing the link and adding an image in the code as a fallback.
I made sure to change the link and place an image in the code as a fallback.

cards.note:
**[pv:173]**

~I took care to change the link and putting an image in the code as a fallback~ is incorrect because it mixes an infinitive ('to change') with a gerund ('putting'). The standard structure uses [take care of] followed by gerunds: [changing] and [putting].

cards.sideA: Déjame pegarlo en el chat.

sideB.text: Let me paste it in the chat.
Let me drop it in the chat.
Give me a second to paste it in the chat.

cards.sideA: Este es el código que está siendo usado actualmente.

sideB.text: This is the code currently being used.
This is the code that's currently in use.
This is the code we are currently using.
This is the code that is being used currently.

cards.sideA: Sorry, no te estoy siguiendo / entendiendo.

sideB.text: Sorry, I'm not following you.
Sorry, I don't quite follow.
Sorry, I'm not quite catching what you mean.

cards.sideA: Lo que sucede es que no estamos sincronizados.

sideB.text: The thing is, we are not in sync.
What's happening is that we aren't synchronized.
The issue is that we are out of sync.
What happens is that we are not in sync.
What happens is that we are not synchronized.

cards.note: ~What happens is that we are not sync~ is incorrect because 'sync' in this context is a noun or verb, not an adjective. You must use the prepositional phrase [in sync] or the adjective [synchronized].

cards.sideA: Blue no está sincronizado del todo con soporte.

sideB.text: Blue is not fully in sync with support.
Blue isn't completely synchronized with support.
Blue is not entirely synced up with support.
Blue is not fully synchronized with support.

cards.sideA: ¿Funciona el enlace? Si no, te lo envío de nuevo.

sideB.text: Is the link working? If not, I'll send it to you again.
Does the link work? If it doesn't, I'll resend it.
Is the link working? If not, I can send it again.

cards.note: ~I'll send you again~ is incorrect because it literally implies you are sending the person somewhere. You must include the object pronoun [it] referring to the link, as in [I'll send it to you again].

cards.sideA: ¿Está bien escrito esto?

sideB.text: Is this written correctly?
Is this spelled right?
Did I write this correctly?
Is this well written?
Is this written well?

cards.sideA: Podríamos delimitar los alcances y limitaciones.

sideB.text: We could define the scope and limitations.
We could outline the scope and boundaries.
We could narrow down the scope and limitations.
We could delimit the scope and limitations.

cards.sideA: El fabricante entregó el producto hoy.

sideB.text: The manufacturer delivered the product today.
The product was delivered by the manufacturer today.
The maker dropped off the product today.

cards.sideA: El maestro cervecero trabaja con pasión.

sideB.text: The master brewer works with passion.
The brewmaster works passionately.
The brewer works with great passion.

cards.sideA: Este fabricante es muy reconocido en el país.

sideB.text: This manufacturer is very well-known in the country.
This maker is highly recognized across the country.
This manufacturer is widely recognized in the country.

cards.sideA: Nuestro cervecero favorito lanzó una nueva bebida.

sideB.text: Our favorite brewer launched a new drink.
Our favorite brewmaster released a new beverage.
A new drink was launched by our favorite brewer.

cards.sideA: El fabricante cambió el diseño de la caja.

sideB.text: The manufacturer changed the box design.
The maker altered the design of the box.
The manufacturer updated the packaging design.

cards.sideA: La empresa cervecera es de Canadá.

sideB.text: The brewery company is from Canada.
The brewing company is Canadian.
The brewery is based in Canada.

cards.note: ~The brewer company is from Canada~ is unnatural. [Brewer] refers to the person or the standalone business entity, but when acting as an adjective modifying "company," you should use [brewing] (e.g., [The brewing company]). Alternatively, just use the noun [brewery].

cards.sideA: La cervecería es el lugar o la instalación donde se produce la cerveza.

sideB.text: The brewery is the place or facility where beer is produced.
The brewery is the facility where beer is made.
A brewery is the location or facility dedicated to producing beer.

cards.note:
~The brewer is the place or facility where beer is produced~ is incorrect. A [brewer] is the person or business that makes the beer, while the [brewery] is the physical location or facility where the beer is brewed.

~The brewery is the place or installation where beer is produced~ uses [installation], which is an unnatural false friend in this context; [facility] is the correct term for a commercial building. Also, ~The brewery is the place or facility where it is produced the beer~ has incorrect word order. In English, the subject must come before the passive verb, so it must be [where the beer is produced].

cards.sideA: Un cervecero es la persona que elabora la cerveza o la empresa que actúa como fabricante.

sideB.text: A brewer is the person who makes the beer or the company that acts as the manufacturer.
A brewer refers to the person brewing the beer or the manufacturing company.
A brewer is the individual who brews the beer or the company acting as the manufacturer.
A brewer is the person who makes the beer or the company that acts as a maker.

cards.sideA: El SKU es el item number que vemos en la pagina web o mobile app.

sideB.text: The SKU is the item number we see on the website or mobile app.
The SKU corresponds to the item number displayed on the website or mobile app.
The SKU is the item number that appears on the website or mobile app.

cards.sideA: Déjame revisar el horario en el calendario.

sideB.text: Let me check the schedule on the calendar.
Let me review the schedule in the calendar.
Let me take a look at the calendar for the schedule.

cards.sideA: ¿Cómo va el desarrollo de la página estática?

sideB.text: How is the static page coming along?
How is the development of the static page going?
How is the progress on the static page?
How is the static page going?

cards.note:
**[pv:201]**

~How is going the development of the static page?~ has incorrect word order. In questions, the subject must come before the continuous verb: [How is the development... going?].

cards.sideA: ¿Cómo va eso?

sideB.text: How is that going?
How is that coming along?
How are things going with that?

cards.sideA: En el catálogo se muestran sub secciones.

sideB.text: Sub-sections are shown in the catalog.
The catalog displays sub-sections.
There are sub-sections shown in the catalog.
Sub-sections are displayed in the catalog.

cards.note: ~In the catalog are shown sub-sections~ is not a natural word order in English. You must either use the passive voice properly ([Sub-sections are shown in the catalog]) or use a dummy subject ([There are sub-sections shown in the catalog]).

cards.sideA: En la lupa se muestra el fabricante del producto.

sideB.text: The product manufacturer is shown in the search bar.
The product manufacturer is shown using the magnifying glass.
The magnifying glass displays the product's manufacturer.
The manufacturer of the product is displayed in the magnifying glass.

cards.sideA: Soy de Chile en Latinoamérica.

sideB.text: I am from Chile in Latin America.
I'm from Chile, in Latin America.
I come from Chile, Latin America.

cards.sideA: Está obsoleta esa dependencia, por eso la cambiamos.

sideB.text: That dependency is deprecated, which is why we changed it.
That dependency is obsolete, so we replaced it.
That dependency is outdated, that's why we changed it.

cards.sideA: Se necesita establecer ese atributo o parámetro en el workflow.

sideB.text: That attribute or parameter needs to be set in the workflow.
It is necessary to set that attribute or parameter in the workflow.
You need to establish that attribute or parameter in the workflow.

cards.sideA: ¿Alguna novedad sobre cómo proceder con el #71?

sideB.text: Any updates on how to proceed with #71?
Do you have any updates on how to proceed with #71?
Are there any updates on how to move forward with #71?
Any news on how to proceed with #71?

cards.sideA: Necesitamos un dropdown con un multiplicador de 1.

sideB.text: We need a dropdown with a multiplier of 1.
We require a dropdown menu with a multiplier of 1.
We need a select dropdown with a multiplier of 1.

cards.sideA: Soy el que hace que las cosas sucedan.

sideB.text: I am the one who gets things done.
I'm the one who makes things happen.
I am the person who gets things done.

cards.sideA: Se te escucha entrecortado (hablando en una reunión).

sideB.text: You are breaking up.
Your audio is cutting out.
You are cutting out a bit.
Your voice is cutting out.

cards.note:
**[pv:11]**

**[pv:44]**

**[pv:200]**

~You are cutting off~ and ~You sound a bit cut off~ are not natural for a choppy connection; [cut off] implies the call was completely disconnected or someone was interrupted. Also, ~You sound a bit cut out~ is incorrect because we use the active continuous form [cutting out] to describe a poor signal (e.g., [You are cutting out]).

cards.sideA: Suele cashear las búsquedas y es más rápida.

sideB.text: It usually caches the searches and is faster.
It tends to cache queries, making it faster.
It often caches the searches and runs quicker.
It tends to cache queries, and it's faster.
It tends to cache the queries, and it is faster.

cards.sideA: Tiende a hacerlo más rápido en producción.

sideB.text: It tends to do it faster in production.
It tends to process it faster in production.
It usually runs faster in production.

cards.sideA: Solo faltan 2 fixes.

sideB.text: There are only 2 fixes left.
We only have 2 fixes to go.
Only 2 fixes remain.
We only have 2 fixes left.

cards.note:
**[er:108]**

~We only lack 2 fixes~ is unnatural. The verb [lack] means a deficiency or absence of something essential (e.g., [We lack resources]), not a countdown of remaining tasks. Use [have... left].

cards.sideA: Solo nos faltan 3 fixes para terminar.

sideB.text: We only have 3 fixes left to finish.
There are only 3 fixes left before we are done.
We are just 3 fixes away from being done.

cards.sideA: Necesito la guía de Sean para este fix.

sideB.text: I need Sean's guidance for this fix.
I need Sean's input on this fix.
I need Sean to walk me through this fix.

cards.note: ~I need the guidance of Sean for this fix~ is grammatically correct but unnatural. For human possession, you must use the Saxon Genitive ([Sean's guidance]). Use 'of' primarily for objects or abstract concepts.

cards.sideA: Necesito la guía de Sean para resolver este problema.

sideB.text: I need Sean's guidance to solve this problem.
I need Sean's help to work through this problem.
I need Sean to guide me in solving this problem.

cards.note: ~I need the guidance of Sean to solve this problem~ is unnatural. In English, human possession is expressed with an apostrophe 's. You should say [Sean's guidance].

cards.sideA: Descomenta esas lineas, y luego comenta las otras de aca.

sideB.text: Uncomment those lines, and then comment out the others here.
Go ahead and uncomment those lines, then comment out the others.
Uncomment those lines, and then comment the other ones out.

cards.note:
**[er:221]**

~Uncomment those lines, and then comment the others here~ is incorrect. You must use the phrasal verb [comment out] to refer to disabling code, not just [comment].

cards.sideA: Bueno, hasta ahora siempre espero el visto bueno.

sideB.text: Well, up until now I always wait for the green light.
Well, up to now I have always waited for approval.
Well, so far I have always waited for the go-ahead.
Well, so far I always wait for the green light.

cards.sideA: Es necesario seguir el procedimiento correcto.

sideB.text: It is necessary to follow the proper procedure.
We need to follow the correct procedure.
You must follow the correct procedure.

cards.sideA: Asegurate de que los datos se guarden correctamente.

sideB.text: Ensure that the data is saved properly.
Make sure the data is saved correctly.
Make sure that the data gets saved correctly.

cards.sideA: Como France lo solicito.

sideB.text: As France requested.
Just as France requested.
As per France's request.
As France asked.

cards.note: ~As France asked for~ is unnatural here. The verb [ask] means to request an action, which fits perfectly ([As France asked]). [Ask for] requires an object (e.g., [As France asked for it]), but in this structure, just [ask] is preferred.

cards.sideA: Nosotros notamos este error hace unos dias.

sideB.text: We noticed this error a few days ago.
We spotted this bug a few days ago.
We caught this error a few days ago.

cards.sideA: Ya no estamos mostrando el signo de exclamacion, solo el fallback img.

sideB.text: We are no longer showing the exclamation mark, just the fallback image.
We are no longer displaying the exclamation sign, only the fallback image.
The exclamation mark is no longer showing, only the fallback image.

cards.sideA: No estamos apuntando a Produccion, sino a Soporte.

sideB.text: We are not targeting Production, but rather Support.
We are not aiming for Production, but rather for Support.
We are not aiming for Production, but for Support.

cards.sideA: Y lo ultimo que supe fue que ese era el link correcto.

sideB.text: And the last thing I heard was that that was the correct link.
And the last I knew, that was the right link.
The last I heard was that that was the correct link.
And the last thing I knew was that that was the correct link.
And the last I knew was that was the correct link.

cards.sideA: En vez de estar al final, ahora esta al comienzo.

sideB.text: Instead of being at the end, it is now at the beginning.
Rather than being at the end, it is now at the beginning.
It used to be at the end, but now it is at the beginning.

cards.sideA: Ahi es lo mismo que antes.

sideB.text: That is the same as before.
It is the same as it was before.
That is just the same as before.

cards.note: ~There is the same as before~ is incorrect. [There] refers to a physical location. To say "ahí" in the abstract sense of "in that aspect" or "in that case", you must use [That] (e.g., [That is the same...]).

cards.sideA: Lo testie y funciona.

sideB.text: I tested it and it works.
I tested it and it is working.
I ran a test on it and it works.

cards.sideA: Lo testie y no funciona.

sideB.text: I tested it and it doesn't work.
I tested it and it is not working.
I ran a test on it and it doesn't work.

cards.sideA: Solo debes descomentar esa linea please.

sideB.text: You just need to uncomment that line, please.
Just uncomment that line, please.
All you need to do is uncomment that line, please.

cards.sideA: Solo debes comentar esa linea, y descomentar la otra.

sideB.text: You just need to comment out that line and uncomment the other one.
Just comment out that line and uncomment the other one.
All you need to do is comment out that line and uncomment the other.

cards.note:
**[er:221]**

~You just need to comment that line and uncomment the other one~ is incorrect in a programming context. The verb [comment] means to express an opinion. To disable a line of code, you must use the phrasal verb [comment out].

cards.sideA: Este procedimiento fue completamente reescrito.

sideB.text: This procedure was completely rewritten.
This procedure has been completely rewritten.
The procedure was entirely rewritten.
